name: Mammoth Creek
short_description: BLM public land rockhounding along Mammoth Creek with excellent agate and chalcedony specimens. Free access, beginner-friendly collecting in scenic Garfield County terrain.
description:
Mammoth Creek flows through public BLM lands in Garfield County, offering accessible rockhounding opportunities for collectors of all skill levels. The creek and surrounding washes expose colorful agate nodules and chalcedony specimens that have weathered from the volcanic and sedimentary formations that dominate this region of south-central Utah. This is a no-permit-required site open to personal recreational collecting, making it an excellent choice for families and beginners exploring Utah's gem potential without navigating complex access rules.

The geology here reflects the complex volcanic and hydrothermal history of the Colorado Plateau. Miocene-age volcanic rocks and silica-rich deposits have created ideal conditions for agate and chalcedony formation. Weathering and erosion along Mammoth Creek have exposed these gem-bearing deposits, concentrating specimens in creek gravels and eroded hillside exposures. The surrounding landscape displays the high desert character typical of central Utah, with pinyon-juniper woodland and scattered sagebrush interspersed with exposed rock outcrops.

Collecting at Mammoth Creek involves walking creek washes and examining exposed gravels for agate nodules, typically ranging from marble to fist-sized. The best specimens often display banding, color variation from cream to rust to gray, and translucent chalcedony characteristics. Unlike fee-dig operations, you're searching natural deposits where find rates depend on water flow history and seasonal exposure changes. The setting is remote enough to feel like a true field experience while remaining relatively accessible from the nearby town of Panguitch.

Water flow varies seasonally, with spring runoff creating larger washes and exposing fresh material. Summer and fall typically offer the best exploring conditions with stable water levels and comfortable collecting weather. The site requires moderate hiking ability and comfort with variable terrain, but rewards effort-minded rockhounds with the satisfaction of discovering specimens in their natural context.
history: Mammoth Creek sits within the Colorado Plateau geological province, where Miocene-age volcanism deposited silica-rich fluids and ash flows that later altered into agate- and chalcedony-bearing formations. Weathering and stream erosion over millions of years have concentrated these specimens in creek gravels and exposed hillside deposits, creating the natural collecting opportunities visible today.
tips: Focus your search in and around the main creek channel and tributary washes where water has concentrated heavier minerals and washed away finer material - look for agate and chalcedony specimens that appear as smooth, rounded nodules with waxy or translucent luster. Bring a small pry bar or rock hammer to carefully extract specimens from clay and gravelly matrix without damaging them. Early morning or after seasonal water events offer the best visibility for spotting fresh material that has been newly exposed by erosion. Screen or pan lighter gravels in the creek itself to find smaller high-quality chalcedony pieces you might otherwise overlook.
terrain_notes: Elevation approximately 7,000 to 7,500 feet. Terrain consists of creek bottoms with moderate gradient and rocky, uneven footing, surrounded by sage-covered hillsides with exposed rock outcrops. Vegetation includes pinyon pine, juniper, and sagebrush. Walking distance from parking varies depending on access point, typically 0.5 to 2 miles of hiking through variable terrain with loose rocks common underfoot.
best_season: April - October. Late spring through early fall offers the most stable collecting conditions with moderate temperatures and manageable water levels. Summer heat can be intense at elevation, making early morning collection preferable. Fall provides excellent weather and lower water levels that expose creek channel material. Winter snow and ice make access difficult.
written_directions: From Panguitch, take US 89 south for approximately 12 miles toward Kanab. Look for BLM access points along Mammoth Creek drainage on the east side of the highway. The most accessible area requires turning east onto forest service or BLM roads near mile marker 93. A high-clearance vehicle is recommended, though passenger cars may reach lower access points during dry conditions. Park in available pullouts near the creek and walk upstream or downstream to begin collecting. Ask locally in Panguitch for current road conditions and specific access gate information.
road_conditions: Dirt and gravel BLM roads with possible washboard surface. High-clearance vehicle recommended but not always necessary. Check road conditions before travel, especially after heavy rain.
parking_notes: Informal pullout parking along BLM roads near Mammoth Creek. No developed parking area. Limited capacity, typically 2-4 vehicles per pullout location.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. Respect natural resource protections and existing wildlife. Pack out all trash.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
Respect BLM resource protections
Pack out all trash
nearest_services: Panguitch, approximately 12 miles northwest. Full services including gas, food, lodging, and water available. Kanab, approximately 25 miles south, offers additional services.
accessibility_notes: Moderate terrain with uneven, rocky creek bottoms and hillside walking. Variable footing and loose rocks require sturdy hiking boots. Creek crossing may be necessary depending on water flow. Not wheelchair accessible. Strenuous hiking not required but good physical fitness helpful for exploring upper creek sections.

faq.question: What gemstones can I find rockhounding at Mammoth Creek near Panguitch, Utah?
faq.answer: Mammoth Creek is best known for agate and chalcedony specimens in various colors ranging from cream and white to rust and gray tones. You'll also find quartz and jasper in the creek gravels and hillside exposures. Most specimens are marble to fist-sized, though careful searching can yield smaller high-quality pieces.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound at Mammoth Creek on BLM land in Utah?
faq.answer: No permit is required for recreational collecting at Mammoth Creek. Utah BLM land allows personal use rockhounding without formal permission, making this a great free access site for casual collectors. You are limited to 25 lbs per day and personal use only - no commercial collection.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to collect agate at Mammoth Creek?
faq.answer: April through October offers the best rockhounding conditions. Late spring and early fall provide moderate temperatures and stable water levels that expose creek channel material. Summer can be quite hot at 7,000+ feet elevation, so early morning collection is recommended. Winter snow and ice typically make access difficult.

faq.question: Is Mammoth Creek rockhounding area good for beginners and families with kids?
faq.answer: Yes, Mammoth Creek is beginner and family-friendly. The site requires no permit, has free public access, and moderate terrain suitable for kids aged 8 and up. The creek-bottom setting with obvious agate nodules makes it easier to learn specimen identification compared to more difficult backcountry locations. Bring plenty of water and sun protection.

faq.question: What tools and gear do I need for rockhounding at Mammoth Creek in Garfield County?
faq.answer: Bring a small pry bar or rock hammer to extract specimens from gravelly matrix, a collection bag for your 25 lb daily limit, sturdy hiking boots for uneven creek terrain, plenty of water, sun protection, and a hand lens or small magnifier to examine specimens. A classifier or small screen helps identify quality pieces in creek gravels.

faq.question: How do I get to Mammoth Creek rockhounding area from Panguitch?
faq.answer: From Panguitch, drive south on US 89 for approximately 12 miles toward Kanab. Look for BLM access points along Mammoth Creek on the east side of the highway near mile marker 93. Turn onto forest service or BLM roads and park in pullout areas near the creek. A high-clearance vehicle is recommended. Call the Panguitch BLM office for current road conditions and specific access information.

meta_description: Free BLM rockhounding at Mammoth Creek for agate and chalcedony near Panguitch. No permit required. Beginner-friendly collecting with scenic terrain.
